title: Rendering Pipeline: Views & Compose
description: Choreographer and VSYNC, View measure/layout/draw, hardware acceleration, RenderThread, Compose LayoutNode tree, Compose three phases, and architecture comparison

references.title: Android Developers — How Android Draws Views

lesson:
The Frame: VSYNC to Pixels

Every frame on Android begins with a VSYNC signal from the display hardware. The Choreographer listens for VSYNC and dispatches frame callbacks in a fixed order. At 60 fps, each frame has a 16.67 ms budget — exceed it and the frame is dropped ("jank").

VSYNC Timeline (60 fps = 16.67ms per frame) VSYNC #1 VSYNC #2 VSYNC #3 │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐┌─────────────────┐┌─────────────────┐ │ Frame N ││ Frame N+1 ││ Frame N+2 │ │ ││ ││ │ │ 1. INPUT ││ 1. INPUT ││ │ │ 2. ANIMATION ││ 2. ANIMATION ││ (dropped!) │ │ 3. TRAVERSAL ││ 3. TRAVERSAL ││ Work from N+1 │ │ (measure/ ││ (measure/ ││ overran into │ │ layout/ ││ layout/ ││ this slot │ │ draw) ││ draw) ││ │ └─────────────────┘└─────────────────┘└─────────────────┘ ◀── 16.67ms ──────▶◀── 16.67ms ──────▶

Choreographer dispatches three callback types each frame:

  • CALLBACK_INPUT — touch events, key events
  • CALLBACK_ANIMATION — ValueAnimator ticks, Compose animations
  • CALLBACK_TRAVERSAL — ViewRootImpl.performTraversals() (measure, layout, draw)
Choreographer.postFrameCallback(): Schedules work on the next VSYNC. This is what powers View.postOnAnimation() and how the framework schedules traversals. If nothing is dirty, no traversal callback is posted — the system is idle.

View Drawing: Measure / Layout / Draw

When a View is invalidated or layout is requested, ViewRootImpl.performTraversals() runs a three-pass traversal of the View tree during the TRAVERSAL callback:

ViewRootImpl.performTraversals() │ ├──▶ 1. performMeasure() ← "How big should each View be?" │ │ │ └──▶ View.measure() → onMeasure() │ Walks tree top-down, parents constrain children │ Result: measuredWidth / measuredHeight │ ├──▶ 2. performLayout() ← "Where should each View go?" │ │ │ └──▶ View.layout() → onLayout() │ Parents assign (left, top, right, bottom) to children │ Result: final position in window │ └──▶ 3. performDraw() ← "What does each View look like?" │ └──▶ View.draw() → onDraw() Canvas operations: drawRect, drawText, drawBitmap Result: DisplayList / RenderNode

MeasureSpec

Parents pass MeasureSpec to children during measure, encoding a mode and a size:

ModeMeaningExample
EXACTLYParent determined exact sizelayout_width="200dp" or match_parent
AT_MOSTChild can be up to this sizewrap_content inside bounded parent
UNSPECIFIEDNo constraintswrap_content inside ScrollView
override fun onMeasure(widthSpec: Int, heightSpec: Int) { val widthMode = MeasureSpec.getMode(widthSpec) val widthSize = MeasureSpec.getSize(widthSpec) val desiredWidth = calculateContentWidth() val width = when (widthMode) { MeasureSpec.EXACTLY -> widthSize // Parent says: be exactly this MeasureSpec.AT_MOST -> min(desiredWidth, widthSize) // Up to this max else -> desiredWidth // UNSPECIFIED: be whatever you want } setMeasuredDimension(width, height) }

requestLayout() vs invalidate()

MethodTriggersWhen to Use
requestLayout()Measure + Layout + DrawSize or position changed
invalidate()Draw onlyAppearance changed (color, text) but size unchanged
Never call requestLayout() from onDraw() or onMeasure(). This creates an infinite loop: measure triggers draw, which triggers measure again. The system will detect this and drop frames or ANR.

Hardware Acceleration & RenderThread

Before API 14, Android used software rendering: the main thread executed Canvas draw operations directly into a bitmap, then copied it to the screen. With hardware acceleration (default since API 14):

Software Rendering (old): Main Thread: onDraw() → Canvas → Bitmap → copy to screen (Everything on main thread, slow) Hardware-Accelerated Rendering (modern): Main Thread: onDraw() → Canvas ops recorded into DisplayList/RenderNode │ ▼ RenderThread: Replays DisplayList → OpenGL ES / Vulkan → GPU → Screen (Separate thread! Main thread is free)

Each View has its own RenderNode containing a DisplayList — a serialized list of Canvas commands. When you call invalidate(), only the dirty View's DisplayList is re-recorded. The RenderThread replays all DisplayLists to produce the frame. This is why invalidate() is cheap with hardware acceleration — other Views' RenderNodes are reused without re-recording.

Hardware layers: View.setLayerType(LAYER_TYPE_HARDWARE, null) renders the View into an off-screen GPU texture. Subsequent frames reuse the texture without re-executing the DisplayList. Ideal for complex Views during animation — but the texture consumes GPU memory, so clear it with LAYER_TYPE_NONE when animation ends.

How Compose Renders

Compose does not create a traditional View hierarchy. Instead, the entire Compose UI lives inside a single AndroidComposeView (which is a View). Composition produces a LayoutNode tree — a lightweight data structure managed by the Compose runtime.

View World: Compose World: ViewRootImpl ViewRootImpl └── FrameLayout └── AndroidComposeView (single View!) ├── LinearLayout └── LayoutNode (root) │ ├── TextView ├── LayoutNode (Column) │ └── ImageView │ ├── LayoutNode (Text) │ │ └── LayoutNode (Image) └── Button └── LayoutNode (Button) N Views = N measure/layout All layout in single-pass Double-taxation possible! No double-taxation

The bridge: AndroidComposeView participates in the View system's measure/layout/draw cycle. When the View system calls its onMeasure()/onLayout()/onDraw(), it delegates to the Compose layout and drawing engine internally.

Compose Three Phases

Compose rendering has three distinct phases, conceptually similar to the View system but architecturally different:

Phase 1: COMPOSITION Phase 2: LAYOUT Phase 3: DRAWING ───────────────────── ────────────────── ──────────────── Runs @Composable functions Measures + places Draws to Canvas Builds/updates LayoutNode LayoutNodes in a via DrawScope tree via slot table SINGLE pass (backed by Skia) │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ Slot Table updated LayoutNode gets Pixels rendered LayoutNodes created/ (width, height, x, y) to screen modified/removed
  1. Composition — Executes your @Composable functions. The Compose compiler plugin transforms them to update a slot table, which tracks the LayoutNode tree. On recomposition, only changed parts of the tree are updated (positional memoization).
  2. Layout — Measures and places every LayoutNode in a single pass. Unlike the View system, Compose enforces that each node is measured exactly once — no double-taxation. IntrinsicMeasurements let you query min/max sizes without violating this rule.
  3. Drawing — Each LayoutNode draws itself via DrawScope, which wraps a Skia Canvas. Drawing commands are recorded into the View system's DisplayList through AndroidComposeView.
Phase independence: Each phase can run independently. If state read during Drawing changes, only Drawing re-runs — Composition and Layout are skipped. Modifier.graphicsLayer { } reads state only during Drawing, so animating alpha, rotation, or scale with graphicsLayer skips Composition and Layout entirely.
// BAD: alpha change triggers Composition + Layout + Drawing var alpha by remember { mutableFloatStateOf(1f) } Box(modifier = Modifier.alpha(alpha)) // alpha read in Composition phase // GOOD: alpha change triggers Drawing only var alpha by remember { mutableFloatStateOf(1f) } Box(modifier = Modifier.graphicsLayer { this.alpha = alpha }) // read in Drawing phase

Views vs Compose Architecture Comparison

AspectView SystemCompose
UI treeView hierarchy (N View objects)LayoutNode tree inside single AndroidComposeView
MeasureMulti-pass allowed (double-taxation)Single-pass enforced (one measure per node)
Layout + DrawSeparate passes via performTraversalsThree phases: Composition, Layout, Drawing
RenderingDisplayList per View + RenderThreadDrawScope records into DisplayList via AndroidComposeView
Partial updateinvalidate() re-records one RenderNodePhase skipping (graphicsLayer skips Composition + Layout)
BridgeN/AAndroidComposeView is a single View in the View system
Mixed layouts hurt: Nesting ComposeView inside RecyclerView items means each item creates a separate AndroidComposeView + composition. This duplicates Compose runtime overhead per item. Prefer a fully Compose LazyColumn or use AbstractComposeView with careful ViewCompositionStrategy.

questions.title: performTraversals() Three Phases
questions.description: What is the correct order of the three passes in ViewRootImpl.performTraversals()?
options: Draw → Layout → Measure
Layout → Measure → Draw
Measure → Layout → Draw
Measure → Draw → Layout
questions.explanation: performTraversals() always runs in the order: Measure → Layout → Draw. First, the tree is measured top-down (parents constrain children with MeasureSpec). Then, parents assign positions to children in the layout pass. Finally, each View draws itself via Canvas. This order is fixed — you cannot change it.
hints: Think about what information each pass needs from the previous one
You need to know size before you can assign position, and position before you can draw

questions.title: requestLayout() in onDraw()
questions.description: What happens when this custom View is displayed?
options: Displays the frame count and updates it once per second
Creates an infinite traversal loop: requestLayout() schedules measure+layout+draw, which calls onDraw() again, which calls requestLayout() — causing continuous jank and potential ANR
Throws an IllegalStateException because requestLayout() cannot be called from onDraw()
Works fine — requestLayout() is asynchronous so it runs on the next frame without issues
questions.explanation: requestLayout() marks the View as needing measure+layout+draw, scheduling a new traversal on the next VSYNC. Each traversal calls onDraw(), which calls requestLayout() again — creating an infinite loop. Every single frame triggers a full measure+layout+draw traversal. The View tree never reaches a stable state, causing constant work on the main thread, dropped frames, and potential ANR. Use invalidate() if you only need to redraw, and never trigger layout from draw.
hints: requestLayout() schedules a full traversal including draw — what calls onDraw()?
Think about what happens when the traversal from requestLayout() reaches the draw phase

questions.title: requestLayout() vs invalidate()
questions.description: A custom View changes its background color but its size and position remain the same. Which method should you call to update the display?
options: requestLayout() — because the View's appearance changed
invalidate() — because only drawing needs to re-run, not measure or layout
forceLayout() — because it's the most efficient option
postInvalidateOnAnimation() — because it skips measure and layout and waits for VSYNC
questions.explanation: invalidate() marks the View as needing only a redraw (the draw pass). Since the size and position haven't changed, there's no need to re-measure or re-layout the entire tree. requestLayout() would trigger an unnecessary measure+layout pass on the whole tree up to the root. With hardware acceleration, invalidate() only re-records the View's DisplayList — very cheap. postInvalidateOnAnimation() is also valid but schedules for the next VSYNC; invalidate() already batches to the next traversal.
hints: What triggers measure+layout+draw vs just draw?
If only the appearance changes (not size), which passes do you actually need?

questions.title: Custom View onMeasure()
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to correctly implement onMeasure() for a custom View that respects parent constraints.

blanks.hint: The class that packs mode and size into a single int
The method you must call at the end of onMeasure() to store the result

questions.explanation: MeasureSpec encodes both a mode (EXACTLY, AT_MOST, UNSPECIFIED) and a size into a single int. You extract them with MeasureSpec.getMode() and MeasureSpec.getSize(). After computing the final dimensions, you must call setMeasuredDimension(width, height) — failing to do so throws an IllegalStateException.
hints: The parent packs constraints into a single integer — which class encodes that?
onMeasure() must end by storing the final width and height

questions.title: Choreographer and VSYNC
questions.description: What is the role of Choreographer in the Android rendering pipeline, and what does the VSYNC-app signal trigger?
options: Choreographer manages the GPU thread pool and VSYNC triggers shader compilation
Choreographer receives VSYNC signals and dispatches frame callbacks in order: INPUT → ANIMATION → TRAVERSAL, coordinating all per-frame work on the main thread
Choreographer runs on the RenderThread and VSYNC triggers DisplayList replay
Choreographer batches multiple frames together and VSYNC triggers buffer swaps on the display
questions.explanation: Choreographer is the main thread's frame scheduler. It registers for VSYNC signals via SurfaceFlinger. When VSYNC-app fires, Choreographer dispatches callbacks in order: CALLBACK_INPUT (touch/key events) → CALLBACK_ANIMATION (ValueAnimator ticks) → CALLBACK_TRAVERSAL (ViewRootImpl.performTraversals). This ensures input is processed before animations, and animations before layout/draw. If no callbacks are pending, Choreographer doesn't request VSYNC — saving power.
hints: Choreographer runs on the main thread, not the RenderThread
The callback order matters: input before animation before layout/draw

questions.title: Modifier.graphicsLayer Phase Skipping
questions.description: How does Compose handle the animated alpha in this code? Which phases run when alpha changes?
options: All three phases run: Composition, Layout, and Drawing — because the state changed
Only Composition and Drawing run — Layout is skipped because size didn't change
Only Drawing runs — the state is read inside graphicsLayer{} which executes only in the Drawing phase, so Composition and Layout are skipped
No phases run — graphicsLayer caches the result and handles animation internally
questions.explanation: Modifier.graphicsLayer { } is a Drawing-phase-only modifier. The lambda inside it executes during the Drawing phase, not during Composition. When alpha changes, Compose sees that the state is only read in Drawing — so it skips Composition (no @Composable functions re-run) and Layout (no re-measurement). Only the Drawing phase runs to apply the new alpha. This is why graphicsLayer is the preferred way to animate visual properties like alpha, rotation, scale, and translation.
hints: Where is the state value actually read — during Composition or inside the graphicsLayer lambda?
Compose tracks which phase reads each state value

questions.title: Compose Single-Pass Layout
questions.description: How does Compose avoid the "double-taxation" problem that affects the View system's measure pass?
options: Compose caches all measurements from the previous frame and reuses them
Compose uses parallel threads to measure different branches simultaneously

Compose enforces that each LayoutNode is measured exactly once per layout pass — multiple measurements throw an exception — and provides intrinsic measurements as a separate pre-pass for cases that need size information
Compose skips the measure pass entirely and uses fixed sizes for all components
questions.explanation: In the View system, a parent can measure a child multiple times (e.g., LinearLayout with layout_weight measures twice). Deeply nested weighted layouts cause exponential measure calls — double-taxation. Compose forbids this: each LayoutNode is measured exactly once. Attempting to measure a node twice throws an exception. For cases where a parent needs to know a child's preferred size before measuring, Compose provides intrinsic measurements (minIntrinsicWidth, maxIntrinsicHeight, etc.) as a separate, cheap query that doesn't count as a real measurement.
hints: What causes double-taxation in Views? LinearLayout with weights...
Compose has a mechanism for querying size without triggering a full measure

questions.title: View Hierarchy vs LayoutNode Tree
questions.description: Explain the key architectural difference between how the View system and Compose manage their UI trees, and how they bridge together.
questions.answer: The View system creates a hierarchy of View objects — each View is a separate object that participates in measure/layout/draw traversals. Compose replaces this with a LayoutNode tree inside a single AndroidComposeView. AndroidComposeView is the bridge: it is one View in the View hierarchy that internally manages the entire Compose LayoutNode tree, delegating View system callbacks to the Compose layout and drawing engine.
questions.explanation: In the View system, N UI elements means N View objects, each with its own measure/layout/draw overhead. In Compose, the entire UI is a tree of lightweight LayoutNode objects inside one AndroidComposeView. This View participates in the traditional View traversal (performTraversals calls its onMeasure/onLayout/onDraw), but internally it runs Compose's own single-pass layout and Skia-based drawing. This architecture means Compose UI is invisible to the View system's layout inspector as individual Views — it's all one View.
hints: How many Views does a Compose screen create in the View hierarchy?
What class bridges the View system and Compose's LayoutNode tree?

questions.title: Missing Hardware Layer for Animation
questions.description: This custom View animates a complex drawing (drop shadow, paths, gradients) every frame. Users report severe jank during the animation. Fix it by leveraging hardware layers.

checks.message: Use a hardware layer during the animation to cache the rendering as a GPU texture
Clear the hardware layer when animation ends to free GPU memory

questions.explanation: Without a hardware layer, every invalidate() re-executes all expensive Canvas operations (shadows, gradients, paths) to rebuild the DisplayList. With LAYER_TYPE_HARDWARE, the View is rendered once into a GPU texture. Subsequent frames apply transformations (alpha, translation, scale) to the cached texture without re-drawing — dramatically cheaper. Set LAYER_TYPE_NONE when the animation ends to free the GPU texture memory. Hardware layers are ideal for short animations on complex Views.
hints: The expensive drawing doesn't need to happen every frame during animation
A hardware layer caches the View as a GPU texture — transforms are cheap on textures
Don't forget to release the GPU texture when the animation finishes

questions.title: ComposeView in RecyclerView
questions.description: When using ComposeView inside RecyclerView item layouts, what happens during the View system's traversal?
options: Each ComposeView shares a single composition, so traversal is efficient

Each ComposeView creates its own AndroidComposeView and separate composition — the View system traverses each as an individual View, and each runs its own Compose phases independently, adding per-item overhead
ComposeView items are automatically batched into a single AndroidComposeView by the framework
RecyclerView disables View traversal for ComposeView items and delegates entirely to Compose
questions.explanation: Each ComposeView in a RecyclerView item creates a separate AndroidComposeView with its own composition, slot table, and LayoutNode tree. The View system's performTraversals() walks through each ComposeView as a normal View — calling onMeasure/onLayout/onDraw, each of which triggers that item's Compose phases internally. This means N visible items = N separate compositions with N separate layout passes. For better performance, migrate the entire list to LazyColumn (one composition, one AndroidComposeView) or carefully manage ViewCompositionStrategy to control composition lifecycle.
hints: Each ComposeView is an independent View — what does that mean for traversal?
Compare this to a single LazyColumn which has one AndroidComposeView for all items
